reasoning.overall: This projects as a fairly even London derby, but Crystal Palace get a small edge from Selhurst Park, slightly better defensive balance, and a matchup that can trouble West Ham in transition and on second balls. Palace are likelier to defend in a compact mid-block and attack through Eberechi Eze, Adam Wharton progression, and Jean-Philippe Mateta’s box presence, while West Ham still carry threat through Jarrod Bowen, Mohammed Kudus, and James Ward-Prowse set pieces. Head-to-head meetings between these sides are often tight and low-margin, so the forecast leans toward a narrow home result or draw rather than a high-confidence Palace win.
reasoning.t1_result: The win distribution is centered on a one-goal Palace edge because home advantage and defensive structure slightly outweigh West Ham’s attacking talent. Draw remains substantial given derby dynamics, similar squad tiers, and both teams’ capacity to cancel each other out for long stretches.
reasoning.t2_player: Palace are projected in a 3-4-2-1/3-4-3 shape built around Guehi-Lacroix-Andersen behind Wharton and Lerma, with Eze and Sarr supporting Mateta. West Ham are projected in a 4-2-3-1 with Bowen, Paquetá, and Kudus around Füllkrug, maximizing wide isolation and set-piece delivery.
reasoning.t3_events: Expect a cautious first phase, with the game opening after 25 minutes once Palace’s wing-backs push higher and West Ham begin targeting direct switches to Bowen and Kudus. Most likely event pattern is one goal before halftime or early second half, then tactical substitutions around 60-75 minutes and a late push from the trailing side.
reasoning.t4_stats: Possession should be close, with Palace marginally ahead at home but not dominant because West Ham can sustain spells through Paquetá and Ward-Prowse. Shot volume looks moderate rather than extreme, reflecting two sides more likely to create from transitions, crosses, and set plays than relentless central chance creation.

reasoning.overall: Bayern at the Allianz Arena usually start fast and can control territory, but Real Madrid’s knockout pedigree and transition threat make this close to even. In a first-leg quarter-final, both teams should be cautious enough to keep the score tight, while elite finishers like Kane and Vinícius can decide it with one or two moments. Bayern’s home edge slightly offsets Madrid’s experience and late-game management, so a narrow Bayern win or draw is the most plausible band.
reasoning.t1_result: I lean toward a home win narrowly, but the draw and away win remain live because both sides can score against top opposition. A 1-1 or 2-1 type game fits a match where Bayern have more of the ball and Madrid are more efficient in transition.
reasoning.t2_player: The lineups should feature Bayern’s strongest attacking pieces around Kane, Musiala, and a high-usage winger/fullback structure, while Madrid should center around Vinícius, Bellingham, and a compact midfield. Those players are the most likely scorer and assister candidates because they drive shot volume and final-third creation in big matches.
reasoning.t3_events: Expect a measured first half with the first goal more likely before or around the hour mark rather than very early. Cards are likelier for tactical fouls against counters, and both managers should use 60-75 minute substitutions to refresh wide and midfield legs.
reasoning.t4_stats: Bayern should edge possession and passing, but Madrid’s shot quality may be comparable or better in fewer attacks. That suggests Bayern around the mid-50s in possession, a modest shot advantage, and a small edge in corners, with saves split depending on which side finishes more cleanly.

reasoning.overall: This projects as a very tight elite-level knockout match: Bayern should have more territorial control at Allianz Arena, but Real Madrid’s transition quality, Champions League game management, and individual match-winners keep the tie balanced. Bayern’s home pressure and volume creation slightly lift their win chance, yet Madrid’s counterattacking threat through Vinícius, Bellingham and Mbappé-type central/left combinations makes an away result highly live. Recent H2H history between these clubs is usually open but decided by fine margins, so a one-goal game or draw is more plausible than a blowout.
reasoning.t1_result: Bayern get a modest home edge from venue, likely shot volume, and sustained possession, but Madrid’s efficiency in big European ties narrows the gap. The score distribution centers on 1-1, 2-1, and 1-2 because both teams have enough attacking quality to score while neither is likely to fully suppress the other.
reasoning.t2_player: Both sides are expected to use star-heavy lineups with aggressive wide threats and attacking full-backs. Kane is Bayern’s most reliable finishing outlet, while Vinícius Júnior and Jude Bellingham are Madrid’s likeliest decisive attackers in open play and late-box arrivals.
reasoning.t3_events: Expect Bayern to start faster, with Madrid more dangerous after regains and in the latter part of each half. Tactical fouls and a few bookings are likely in midfield and wide defensive zones, with attacking substitutions around 60–75 minutes if the match is level.
reasoning.t4_stats: Bayern should shade possession and passes at home, but Madrid can match or exceed them in chance quality despite fewer attacks. That points to moderate shot totals for both, several saves, and a corner count slightly favoring Bayern due to territorial pressure.
